title: What is a test in Go
instruction: A test is just a function that checks whether your code does what it should. In Go, tests follow one rule: `func TestXxx(t *testing.T)`. Run this and see a passing test.
successMessage: The test passed — `countBooks()` returned 2 as expected. In a real project, `go test` runs all tests in the package automatically.

title: t.Errorf — report a failure
instruction: `t.Errorf` marks the test as failed and logs a message but the test keeps running. Add a second test case: create an empty library and verify `countBooks()` equals 0.
hint: lib := Library{}  got := lib.countBooks()  if got != 0 { t.Errorf("countBooks() = %d, want 0", got) }

codeChecks.message: Call countBooks() on an empty library and capture the result.
Use t.Errorf to report if the result is wrong.
Replace the TODO comment with your test case.

successMessage: Two cases, one test function. Both pass. t.Errorf reports failures without stopping the whole test.

title: Table-driven tests
instruction: The most common Go testing pattern: define a slice of test cases, loop through them. Add two more cases: searching for "000-000" (not found) and "978-789" (found).
hint: Add {"000-000", nil} and {"978-789", &Book{"Testing In Go", "978-789"}} to the tests slice.

codeChecks.message: Add the found case for ISBN 978-789.
Replace the TODO comment with your two test cases.

successMessage: 4/4 passed. Table-driven tests are the gold standard in Go — add a new case to the table, the loop handles it.

title: t.Run — subtests with names
instruction: `t.Run` gives each test case its own name. When a test fails, you see exactly which case broke. Wrap each case in `t.Run` with the format "lookupByISBN(isbn)".

codeChecks.message: Wrap each test case in t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) { ... }).
Use fmt.Sprintf to create the test name from tt.isbn.
Replace the TODO comment with your t.Run call.

successMessage: Named subtests. When this fails, Go reports exactly which subtest failed. No guessing which input broke.

title: Testing with interfaces — mocking
instruction: Define dependencies as interfaces so you can swap in fake implementations in tests. Run this — `mockService` returns a canned book instead of calling a real API.
successMessage: No real ISBN lookup made. The mock recorded the call and the test verified the behaviour. Interface + fake implementation is how professional Go testing works.

title: Test helpers with t.Helper()
instruction: Extract repeated assertion logic into a helper function and call `t.Helper()` inside it. Add a fourth call that checks "978-789" returns a book titled "Network Programming".

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your assertion.

successMessage: t.Helper() makes failures point to the exact test line, not the helper internals. A small thing that makes debugging much easier.

title: Fix the failing test
instruction: `lookupByISBN` is broken — it compares `Title` instead of `ISBN`. Fix it so all 4 test cases pass.
hint: for _, b := range l.Books { if b.ISBN == isbn { return &b } }  return nil

codeChecks.message: Compare b.ISBN == isbn, not b.Title == isbn.
Return nil if no book is found.

successMessage: 4/4 passed. The test was right — the function was wrong. That is the value of tests: they are the objective referee.

title: Build a tested library catalog
instruction: Build a library catalog with full test coverage. Define `Book` and `Library`, implement `addBook`, `findByISBN`, `listBooks`. Write a table-driven test with `t.Run` covering adding, finding, and not-found cases.
hint: Define a Library struct with a Books slice and methods. Use a table with op string field to distinguish operations.

codeChecks.message: Use t.Run to name each subtest.
Include a not-found test case where findByISBN returns nil.
Print the pass count at the end.

successMessage: A fully tested library catalog. Three methods, named subtests, coverage for found and not-found cases. This is production-quality Go code.
